cases.title: Chest Pain
cases.case_introduction: A 65-year-old white man is brought in to the emergency department because of sharp chest pain and difficulty breathing. At bedside, he currently is in acute distress, and is moaning and holding his hands over the right side of his chest

hpi.history: The patient, a 65-year-old accountant, is brought to the emergency department by ambulance from the trucking company, where he works. Ten minutes prior to the ambulance's arrival, the patient developed excruciating, sharp pain on the right side of his chest accompanied by marked respiratory distress. He rates the pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. The pain increases on respiration, and he is unable to answer questions. A coworker who accompanied the patient to the hospital says that this has never happened to the patient in the past. Coworker mentions patient has a past medical history of emphysema and asthma. Oxygen was administered during transport.

answer_key.case_summary: Differential: Aortic dissection, Tension pneumothorax, Asthma exacerbation, Pneumothorax, Pulmonary embolism, Angina. The patient has a tension pneumothorax. This is a medical emergency. No time should be wasted in this case. The tension pneumothorax should be diagnosed through the physical exam and not through radiology. A thoracentesis is a good diagnostic procedure to confirm your physical exam findings of a tension pneumothorax. This is a short term fix and a chest tube must be placed. After a chest tube is placed, it is important to get an X-ray to confirm the correct placement of the chest tube. After the chest tube is in place and the placement is confirmed, the patient should be transferred to the ward for monitoring.

cases.title: Altered Mental Status
correct_orders: CT of head without contrast / MRI, head, without gadolinium
cases.case_summary:
Differential: the differential for altered mental status in an elderly patient includes: seizures, bacterial meningitis, urinary tract infection, trauma, electrolyte disturbances, drug or alcohol toxicity, heart failure, metabolic disorders: hypoglycemia, hypercalcemia, uremia, liver failure, thyrotoxicosis.

The patient has a substantial hemorrhage. The clerk is told the patient had a recent fall and has been having episodes of altered mental status since that time. The patient is also at a higher risk for subdural hematomas due to his advanced age and having a recent alcohol intoxication. Especially with falls in elderly, it is important to look for head trauma. CT without contrast is more widely used for the swift turn-around time for results, but MRI is more sensitive in detecting intracranial hemorrhages. Time is of utmost importance in this case as bleeds may cause mass effects that may lead to increased brain damage as time elapses.

should_have_ordered: Advise patient, no illegal drug use
cases.case_summary:
Differential: There is a large differential for a right adnexal mass in a reproductive age female. This could include ectopic pregnancy, tubo-ovarian abscess, inflammatory bowel disease, ovarian mass, endometrioma, theca lutein cysts, germ cell neoplasms, metastatic disease, and many others.

The patient has an endometrioma. She is having severe pelvic pain which worsens with sexual activity. Typically a pelvic ultrasound is performed, but a CT scan or a MRI of the abdomen/pelvis area would also be acceptable to find the endometrioma. This can be removed by ordering a laparotomy. It is also acceptable to consult OB/GYN who can do the laparotomy. Once this is done, the case will end. You can then do preventative measures of counseling regarding smoking cessation and illicit drug use.

cases.title: Intra Abdominal Hypertension
cases.case_summary: Intra-abdominal hypertension (IAH) is defined as an intra-abdominal pressure (IAP) greater than 12 mmHg. It is a common complication in critically ill patients, particularly those with trauma, sepsis, or abdominal surgery. IAH can lead to abdominal compartment syndrome (ACS), which can cause organ dysfunction and failure. Early recognition and management of IAH are crucial to prevent progression to ACS. Treatment options include fluid resuscitation, decompression, and monitoring of intra-abdominal pressure.

cases.case_summary:
Differential: Cluster headache, Intracranial neoplasm, Meningitis, Migraine headache, Pseudotumor cerebri, Sinusitis, Tension headache.

The purpose of this case is to have the user identify a benign condition and restrain from doing unnecessary, dangerous procedures. This patient has a simple tension headache, a very common condition that can be diagnosed clinically and treated as an outpatient. The patient describes certain features including mild to moderate pain which is supported by the physical finding of pericranial and neck muscle tenderness. Otherwise, the exam and vital signs are normal. The user should recognize that unnecessary tests, such as "understanding" identity and acute stress, neurologic deficits, nausea/vomiting, or wanting to sleep in the next hour are not helpful diagnostic to ascertain whether this is a tension headache or not. A CT, MRI, or lumbar puncture is not necessary for tension headaches because the patient has no red flags, as stress is a contributing factor in many patients. The patient will get better with simple NSAID therapy. Alternative treatments for tension headaches include relaxation techniques and trigger point injections. However, there are no restrictive treatments and should only be used after the patient has failed these measures.

cases.title: Fever and Rash - Pediatrics
cases.case_summary:
Differential: Fifth disease, Measles, Meningitis, Rubella, Scarlet Fever, Drug allergy

This patient has Roseola infantum (exanthem subitum), most frequently caused by human Herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6). The classic presentation for Roseola is in infants 7-13 months old who develop high fevers for 3-5 days (can often cause febrile seizures) that resolve abruptly, followed by the appearance of a macular rash on the neck and torso, spreading to the extremities. Infants are generally well-appearing, active, and after throughout the course of the illness and treatment is mainly supportive (antipyretics for fever, ensuring adequate fluid intake, etc). The main points of this case is to recognize the clinical presentation and make the diagnosis without any diagnostic tests and manage it conservatively without invasive measures.

This patient has cauda equina syndrome. Cauda equina is characterized by the disruption of the cauda equina, the most distal portion of the spinal cord. Cauda equina syndrome is a medical emergency requiring prompt surgical intervention. Patients suspected of having cauda equina should receive a STAT MRI and neurosurgery consult for spinal decompression. The key to this case is a fast diagnosis with a STAT MRI and a fast consult to neurosurgery. The case will end after neurosurgery sees the patient.

cases.case_summary:
Differentiate: CHF, pneumonia, embolism, aspiration, heart failure, pneumonia, systemic lupus erythematosus.

The patient has fat embolism syndrome (FES). This typically occurs from hours to days after an initial injury that allows globules (typically from bone marrow) to enter the blood stream. Long bones such as a femur are common causes of FES. FES can also cause respiratory distress, and information on risk factors should be taken, including the patient's history.

The patient has many metabolic abnormalities; the lab results and thrombocytopenia which are very common in fat embolism syndrome. He also has metabolic acidosis, increased level of BUN, and increased creatinine which are also commonly seen in fat embolism syndrome.

Diagnosis is mainly clinical as imaging is not very reliable. Chest x-ray and CT scans can be misleading in this case. The patient could have an abnormal CT scan, but this will take too much time to administer. The patient will require immediate supportive treatment.

A commonly used criteria is the Gurd and Wilson criteria for fat embolism to help clinicians determine if the patient is suffering from a fat embolism. The major criteria are:

1. Pyrexia > 38.0 °C
2. Central nervous system depression
3. Petechiae

There are other criteria, but these will not be listed. The patient, positive in the minor criteria, has tachycardia more than 100 beats per minute, and his globules in plasma in the urine. At least two positive major criteria plus one minor criteria is suggestive of fat embolism. In this case, the patient has safety problems, hypotension, and tachycardia; all of these in turn is a concern order on the EMS.

Treatment is supportive. The patient should be transferred to the ICU, intubated, put on a ventilator. The patient should be given fluids. Albumin therapy is considered experimental and corticosteroids administration is controversial, so the majority treatment is just supportive. One thing to avoid is heparin/anticoagulant treatment. This has shown benefit in fat embolism cases, and it increases the risk of hemorrhage.

cases.title: Agitation - Psychiatry
correct_orders: Basic metabolic profile / Comprehensive metabolic profile
should_have_ordered: Electrocardiography, 12 lead / Electrocardiography, ambulatory / Electrocardiography, rhythm strip, long
cases.case_summary:
Differential diagnosis: Substance induced psychotic disorder, Schizophrenia, Psychosis secondary to a medical condition, Delirium, Epileptic seizures, Electrolyte disturbance

This case is similar to the schizophrenia cases, but this case focuses just on correctly treating acute agitation which is a common occurrence in most emergency departments. As the patient has already assaulted a staff member, IV medication is needed for rapid medication onset to be effective. A typical regimen is colloquially called the 5-2-1 regimen 5 mg of Halidol (Haloperidol), 2 mg of IM Ativan (Lorazepam), and 1 mg of IM Cogentin. As Cogentin is key here, an alternative is 2.5 mg of Halidol, 2 mg of IM Ativan (Lorazepam), and 5 mg of IM Benadryl (Diphenhydramine). Droperidol has its limitations as it is not the best choice. Once the patient is treated, the vital signs can be completed. There can be medical causes of acute agitation which should be ruled out, but given the age of the patient and his history in the hospital, it is unlikely that a medical diagnosis will be determined in this case.

Other second-generation antipsychotics are available for the distressed patient including: IM Olanzapine (Zyprexa), IM Aripiprazole (Abilify), or IM Ziprasidone (Geodon). Halidol is the most commonly administered in hospital emergency settings due to the availability of IM administration along with Lorazepam (Ativan) along with Cogentin or Benadryl to avoid a potential EPS (Extrapyramidal Syndrome) and dyskinesias. This is an area which is being studied as newer antipsychotics will treat agitation as well as psychosis.

This patient in this case has immune thrombocytopenia (ITP). The patient most likely developed ITP after having a recent viral infection. ITP patients will have platelet counts less than 100,000/microl, with a normal white blood cell count and hemoglobin. The majority of children develop ITP after a recent viral illness. The patient has gingival bleeding and petechiae. This would classify the patient as a grade I/IV between moderate and severe. The patient will require treatment with either glucocorticoids, IVIG or both. 

The patient at risk for potentially severe bleeding if injured, so the patient should be counseled to avoid contact sports as well as to avoid antiplatelet medications like Aspirin and NSAIDs. Depending on the treatment choice, the patient can be treated either inpatient or outpatient. Once the patient gets treatment, he will improve.

The patient has Ankylosing Spondylitis. The patient has classic symptoms of Ankylosing Spondylitis which include back pain, morning stiffness, and a history of psoriasis. These increase the likelihood of having Spondylitis.

Initial evaluation should be a physical exam focusing on extremities and eyes for iritis/uveitis. Other tests include HLA-B27, and X-rays of the pelvis and spine.

Once the diagnosis of Ankylosing Spondylitis is made, treatment should be with a TNF inhibitors Etanercept, Infliximab, Adalimumab (not an order on the USMLE), but TNF inhibitors in 1 inhibitors are not yet available as orders on the USMLE which include Secukinumab and Ixekizumab. Interleukin 17 inhibitors are also treatment options for Ankylosing Spondylitis.

Once the correct treatment is given, the patient will begin to feel better after a week, and the case will end.

The patient has Acute Epididymitis. Due to the age of the patient and his risk sexual partners, his acute Acute Epididymitis is most likely from an infectious source. Imaging is not required in this case as the Physical exam shows tenderness and swelling of the epididymis. The patient’s clinical picture reflects the quality would be negative in testicular torsion.

The most common infectious causes of Acute Epididymitis are N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis. These should be tested for, but they are commonly negative in those who do not have urinary tract symptoms such as in this case. As the patient practices safe sex, co-infection between the two could be the cause of his Acute Epididymitis.

This case can be treated as an outpatient. The patient can be treated with oral antibiotics, NSAIDS, application of ice to the scrotum, and scrotal elevation.

The antibiotic choice in this case would be ceftriaxone plus a fluoroquinolone to cover all the possible culprits of gonorrhea, chlamydia, and rectal flora.

After 48 hours of treatment, the patient will begin to feel better, and the case will end. The patient should be counseled on safe sex practices.

Differential: Acute Kidney Disease, Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease (ADPKD), Medullary Cystic Kidney Disease, IgA Nephropathy, Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis, Fabry disease.

The patient has end stage renal failure likely from diabetes mellitus and hypertension. The patient is not active with a creatinine of 9.4. The patient has primary metabolic acidosis with an increased anion gap with full exploratory compensation. The patient has too many metabolic abnormalities to be treated as an outpatient (including a possibility of 6), the patient will require inpatient hospitalization and dialysis.

Nephrology should be consulted to help with the management of the patient. A kidney transplant will be needed as a long-term solution.

Indications for dialysis include needing nutritional status, fatigue, muscle mass, and cognitive impairment, hyperkalemia, refractory acidosis. The patient in this case easily meets these criteria.

Once the patient is put on dialysis, the patient will begin to feel better, and the case will end.

We have received emails regarding why this patient requires hemodialysis. The patient is symptomatic and has a creatinine of 9.4. The calculated eGFR based on a 60-year-old would be mL/min/1.73m2. The patient is having memory problems. Your doctor, and is very well right; the patient will require dialysis. Dialysis indications are the following - eGFR is < 15 mL/min/1.73m2, uremic encephalopathy (the patient has been having episodes of confusion which is likely due to uremic encephalopathy), cognitive impairment, hyperkalemia, fatigue and malaise, persistent volume overload, and declining nutritional status. The patient clearly meets these criteria. Efforts to treat the hyperkalemia with various medications prior to hemodialysis are not needed as hemodialysis will treat the hyperkalemia and other metabolic problems.

cases.case_summary: The patient has squamous cell carcinoma of the lip. This is a fairly straight forward case. You diagnose by a biopsy and then remove the tumor by consulting dermatology to perform Mohs surgery. Mohs surgery tends to be the gold standard for removing facial tumors as it minimizes tissue loss. The Mohs surgeon will remove a layer where the tumor was located and take the tissue, dye it, and look at the histology of the removed tissue. If there are still cancer cells in the specimen, the Mohs surgeon will go back and remove more of the lesion and then there is no more cancer. The patient will wait in the office while the Mohs surgeon looks at the tissue, so that the surgeon can remove more tissue if necessary. Mohs surgery will preserve the most tissue and have the best cosmetic outcome which is important to most patients. Mohs surgery is an outpatient procedure and can be done with local anesthetic. Once the surgery is completed, the case will end.

cases.case_summary:
Differential: Beta-ketothiolase deficiency, Glycine encephalopathy, Methylmalonic acidemia, Propionic acidemia.

The patient has the signs of classic Maple Syrup Urine Disease (MSUD) which include irritability, poor feeding, bicycling movements, focal dystonia, and seizures. The are 5 types of MSUD (Classic, Intermediate, Intermittent, Thiamine-responsive, and E3-deficient). Classic MSUD is the most common. The amino acid levels are given in two because these orders are not present on the USMLE at this time. There are no orders for serum or urine levels of leucine, valine, isoleucine, or leucine. However, you must put together that high amino acid levels with the presentation to come up with the MSUD diagnosis.

The treatment for MSUD is a no-protein/low-protein diet. This specialized diet would be life-long much like PKU. The patient would not be able to breast-feed and would need a non-branched-chain amino acids (meaning low, leucine, valine, isoleucine) formula. There is no specific diet for non-branched-chain amino acid diet, so a low-protein/protein diet is utilized. It is recommended that all patients with MSUD be treated with a thiamine to see if the patient has the Thiamine-responsive type. If there is no improvement after 4 weeks, then the thiamine treatment can be discontinued.

A potential long term treatment of MSUD is liver transplantation. As the patient is only a week old, this discussion would happen at a later time and would depend on the outcome of diet modification.

Note that in this case, the CBC/ CMP will be normal which is not abnormal as the patient seems to be suffering more neurological damage than metabolic issues. However, metabolic decompensation is a possibility, so the patient should be followed closely. Admitting to the hospital/ICU is appropriate until the patient can be stabilized and amino acid levels returned to normal levels. MSUD is more severe than PKU as this can lead to death.
